section.title: True Fasting and the Sabbath
section.sectionRationale: This oracle exposes the gap between religious performance and covenant obedience with surgical precision. Israel fasts, bows the head, spreads sackcloth, and asks God why He does not notice; God answers that the fast He has chosen looks like loosing chains, feeding the hungry, and welcoming the homeless. It is one of Scripture's clearest statements that worship without justice is empty.
section.thesis: The fasting God desires is not ritual self-denial but active justice, breaking every yoke, feeding the hungry, sheltering the homeless, and honoring the Sabbath as a delight, and only this kind of worship brings the light, healing, and guidance God promises.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the LORD's charge against empty ritual; the fast God has actually chosen; and the promised blessing of light, healing, and satisfaction for those who honor the Sabbath and pursue justice.

units.label: Why have we fasted, and You have not seen?

verses.text: “Cry aloud, do not hold back! Raise your voice like a ram’s horn. Declare to My people their transgression and to the house of Jacob their sins.

For day after day they seek Me and delight to know My ways, like a nation that does what is right and does not forsake the justice of their God. They ask Me for righteous judgments; they delight in the nearness of God.”
“Why have we fasted, and You have not seen? Why have we humbled ourselves, and You have not noticed?” “Behold, on the day of your fast, you do as you please, and you oppress all your workers.
You fast with contention and strife to strike viciously with your fist. You cannot fast as you do today and have your voice be heard on high.
Is this the fast I have chosen: a day for a man to deny himself, to bow his head like a reed, and to spread out sackcloth and ashes? Will you call this a fast and a day acceptable to the LORD?

units.label: The fast that I have chosen
units.note: break the chains, feed the hungry, clothe the naked

verses.text: Isn’t this the fast that I have chosen: to break the chains of wickedness, to untie the cords of the {{ol|yoke}}, to set the oppressed free and tear off every yoke?
Isn’t it to share your bread with the hungry, to bring the poor and homeless into your home, to clothe the naked when you see him, and not to turn away from your own flesh and blood?
Then your light will break forth like the dawn, and your healing will come quickly. Your righteousness will go before you, and the glory of the LORD will be your rear guard.
Then you will call, and the LORD will answer; you will cry out, and He will say, ‘Here I am.’ If you remove the yoke from your midst, the pointing of the finger and malicious talk,
and if you give yourself to the hungry and satisfy the afflicted soul, then your light will go forth in the darkness, and your night will be like noonday.
The LORD will always guide you; He will satisfy you in a sun-scorched land and strengthen your frame. You will be like a well-watered garden, like a spring whose waters never fail.
Your people will rebuild the ancient ruins; you will restore the age-old foundations; you will be called {{gader|Repairer of the Breach}}, Restorer of the Streets of Dwelling.

If you turn your foot from breaking the Sabbath, from doing as you please on My holy day, if you call the Sabbath a delight, and the LORD’s holy day honorable, if you honor it by not going your own way or seeking your own pleasure or speaking idle words,
then you will delight yourself in the LORD, and I will make you ride on the heights of the land and feed you with the heritage of your father Jacob.” For the mouth of the LORD has spoken.

ol.gloss: Yoke, the wooden bar laid across the neck of a laboring animal or an oppressed person. The fast God chooses tears off every ol, whether the yoke of slavery, injustice, or oppressive speech, verse 9's pointing of the finger; Jesus offers a different yoke entirely, easy and light, Matthew 11:29-30, the rest this yoke-breaking fast anticipates.

gader.gloss: Repairer of the breach, one who rebuilds a broken wall. The title given to those who practice true justice and honor the Sabbath pictures restoration of what sin and neglect have ruined, both the literal ruins of Jerusalem and, by extension, broken relationships and communities repaired through righteous living.

basic.context: The people fast and wonder why God seems not to notice. God answers plainly: their fasting is paired with oppression, exploiting workers and quarreling, so it cannot rise to Him. The fast He actually wants looks like loosing chains, feeding the hungry, and sheltering the homeless, joined with honoring the Sabbath as delight rather than burden.
basic.hermeneutics: This is not a rejection of fasting or the Sabbath as such, but of religious ritual detached from justice and mercy. Isaiah insists that true worship and social righteousness are inseparable; you cannot fast acceptably while oppressing your workers, and you cannot claim to seek God while ignoring the hungry at your door.
basic.originalLanguages: The fast God chooses breaks every ol, yoke, whether of wickedness, oppression, or cruel speech. And those who practice this justice, alongside honoring the Sabbath, earn the title gader poretz, repairer of the breach, rebuilders of what was ruined.
basic.typology: The true fast that frees the oppressed and feeds the hungry anticipates Jesus' own mission statement, to proclaim good news to the poor, to set at liberty those who are oppressed, Luke 4:18, itself drawn from Isaiah 61. The yoke torn off here anticipates the easy yoke Christ offers in its place.
basic.christ: Jesus reads His mission directly out of Isaiah's language of freedom for the oppressed, and in Matthew 25 He identifies Himself with the hungry fed, the naked clothed, and the stranger welcomed, as if done to Him directly, the very acts this chapter names as true fasting.
basic.god: God is not impressed by self-denial disconnected from mercy; He measures worship by whether it produces justice for the vulnerable. Yet He is also generous beyond the demand, promising light, healing, guidance, and satisfaction to those who actually live this way.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 25:35-40 has Jesus identify with the hungry, naked, and stranger cared for. Matthew 11:28-30 offers an easy yoke in place of heavy burdens. James 1:27 defines pure religion as caring for orphans and widows in distress.
basic.oneStory: God has always cared more about hearts turned to justice and mercy than about ritual performed for its own sake. This chapter's true fast, breaking yokes and feeding the hungry, finds its fullest embodiment in Jesus, who both proclaims freedom to the oppressed and identifies Himself with the hungry and homeless He calls His people to serve.

context.literary: Structured as a courtroom-style confrontation, God's people ask why their fasting goes unnoticed, and God answers with a redefinition of true fasting as active justice, followed by conditional promises of light and healing, then a closing Sabbath oracle promising delight for those who honor it rightly.
context.historical: Addressed to a post-exilic community that had resumed formal religious observance, fasting and Sabbath-keeping, while continuing social injustice, oppressing workers and ignoring the poor, exposing that outward religious restoration had not produced inward covenant faithfulness.
context.canonical: Stands alongside other prophetic critiques of empty ritual, Amos 5:21-24, Micah 6:6-8, as one of Scripture's clearest statements that worship divorced from justice and mercy is worthless to God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confront a community confused about why their religious devotion seemed to yield no response from God, and to teach that true fasting and Sabbath-keeping must be joined to active justice and mercy toward the vulnerable, or they are empty.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter is heavily prescriptive, naming specific practices, breaking chains, sharing bread, clothing the naked, honoring the Sabbath as delight, as the concrete shape true worship must take, not merely describing a past situation.

debates.question: Does this passage abolish fasting and Sabbath-keeping as valid religious practices?

views.label: It redefines rather than abolishes them
views.case: The chapter never tells Israel to stop fasting or keeping Sabbath; it insists these practices must be joined to justice and mercy to be acceptable, correcting abuse rather than ending the practice.

views.label: It relativizes ritual in favor of ethics
views.case: Some read the passage as prioritizing ethical action so strongly that ritual observance becomes secondary or symbolic, a a lesser concern compared to justice for the oppressed.

debates.bearing: Either reading agrees on the central point: ritual practice without justice and mercy is worthless to God, and any legitimate use of fasting or Sabbath must produce, not replace, a transformed life of care for the vulnerable.

study.originalLanguages: The fast God chooses tears off every ol, yoke, whether of wickedness or cruel speech. Those who live this way, and who also honor the Sabbath rightly, are called gader poretz, repairer of the breach, people who rebuild what has been broken rather than merely observing rituals over its ruins.

typology.type: Breaking every yoke and freeing the oppressed, verse 6
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' mission statement in Luke 4:18, to proclaim liberty to the captives and set at liberty those who are oppressed.

typology.type: Feeding the hungry, clothing the naked, welcoming the homeless, verse 7
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' identification with the hungry, naked, and stranger in Matthew 25:35-40.

study.christ: Jesus reads His own mission almost word for word out of this section of Isaiah, proclaiming good news to the poor and liberty to the oppressed, Luke 4:18. In Matthew 25 He goes further, identifying Himself personally with the hungry fed, the naked clothed, and the stranger welcomed, as though every act of mercy this chapter demands were done directly to Him. And where this chapter promises a yoke broken, Jesus offers a new yoke in its place, His own, which is easy and whose burden is light, Matthew 11:29-30.
study.god: God is not moved by fasting joined to oppression; He measures the worship He receives by whether it bears fruit in justice for the hungry, the enslaved, and the homeless. Yet His response to genuine obedience is lavish, light breaking forth, healing coming quickly, guidance in the wilderness, and satisfaction like a well-watered garden.

crossReferences.note: Jesus announces His mission using language of freedom for the oppressed drawn from Isaiah.
Jesus identifies with the hungry, naked, and stranger cared for, the very acts this chapter names as true worship.
Christ offers His own easy yoke where this chapter promises every heavy yoke broken.
Pure religion defined as caring for the vulnerable, echoing Isaiah's redefinition of true fasting.

study.oneStory: God has always measured worship by its fruit in justice and mercy, not by its ritual performance alone. This ancient call to break every yoke and feed the hungry finds its full embodiment in Jesus, who proclaims freedom to the oppressed and identifies with the very hungry, naked, and homeless this chapter calls God's people to serve.

quiz.q: Why does God say Israel's fasting in verses 3-5 goes unnoticed?

options.text: Because it is paired with oppressing workers, quarreling, and striking with a wicked fist rather than justice.
options.note: Verses 3-4 name the specific injustice accompanying their fast.

options.text: Because they did not fast for enough days.
options.note: The problem is the injustice accompanying the fast, not its duration.

options.text: Because fasting itself is forbidden by God.
options.note: The chapter sharply critiques their fasting, but it redefines rather than forbids fasting outright.

options.text: Because they fasted in the wrong season of the year.
options.note: Timing is not the issue named in the text; injustice is.

quiz.q: According to verses 6-7, what does the fast God has chosen look like?

options.text: Breaking chains of wickedness, sharing bread with the hungry, and bringing the homeless into your home.
options.note: These verses give a concrete, active description of true fasting as justice and mercy.

options.text: Longer periods of self-denial and stricter sackcloth and ashes.
options.note: This is exactly the old form the chapter questions in verse 5, asking whether that alone constitutes an acceptable fast.

options.text: Avoiding all food and water for extended periods with no other requirement.
options.note: The chosen fast is defined by justice and mercy, not extended abstinence alone.

options.text: Withdrawing from society to focus solely on private prayer.
options.note: The chosen fast is explicitly about active engagement with the needs of others.

quiz.q: What promise is given to those who honor the Sabbath as a delight, verses 13-14?

options.text: They will delight in the LORD, ride on the heights of the land, and be fed with Jacob's heritage.
They will be exempt from all future hardship.
options.note: The promise is real blessing, but it is specific, delight and inheritance, not a blanket exemption from all hardship.

options.text: They will receive no reward, since Sabbath-keeping is purely symbolic.
options.note: The text gives a real, specific promise of blessing.

options.text: They will be forbidden from any rest on other days.
options.note: The passage promotes Sabbath delight, not a prohibition on rest at other times.

quiz.q: How does Jesus' mission statement in Luke 4:18 relate to this chapter?

options.text: It echoes Isaiah's language of freeing the oppressed and breaking every yoke, applying it to His own ministry.
options.note: Jesus draws His mission language from this section of Isaiah's prophecy of liberty for the oppressed.

options.text: It has no connection to this passage.
options.note: The connection in language and theme is direct.

options.text: It reverses Isaiah's meaning to focus only on personal piety.
options.note: Jesus' mission emphasizes active liberation and good news to the poor, matching Isaiah's emphasis.

options.text: It applies only to physical prisoners, not any other kind of oppression.
options.note: Literal captives are included, but the mission statement, like Isaiah's fast, addresses broader oppression, not solely literal imprisonment.

questions: Israel fasted while oppressing their workers and quarreling. Where might your own religious practice, church attendance, prayer, giving, be disconnected from how you actually treat people?

The fast God chose was breaking chains, feeding the hungry, and welcoming the homeless. What is one concrete act of justice or mercy you could do this week rather than simply another private discipline?
Verse 7 says not to turn away from your own flesh and blood. Is there a family member or close relationship you have been neglecting while attending to more distant, easier acts of charity?
God promises light, healing, and guidance to those who live this way. Where do you need that promised light and healing in your own life right now?
The Sabbath is described as a delight, not a burden. Is rest, in whatever form God calls you to it, something you actually delight in, or something you resent or ignore?

translationNotes.note: Cry aloud like a ram's horn renders shophar language elsewhere used for alarm or summons; the prophet is commanded to sound this indictment as urgently as a trumpet call to battle.

Repairer of the Breach and Restorer of the Streets of Dwelling are honorific titles, likely referring to literal rebuilding of Jerusalem's ruined walls and streets as well as the restoration of broken community life.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via makor-scripture toolkit for Isaiah 58:1-14; no em dashes present in this passage.
